title: Coordination Tax

content.statement: As complexity rises, more value gets consumed by coordination itself.
content.question: What % of your work week goes to alignment instead of output? Is that ratio getting worse as you grow?
content.equation: CT = Dependencies × Coord Time × Misalignment Prob × Frequency
sop: Track your hours for one week and label each block as coordination (aligning, syncing, explaining) or output (work actually produced).
Calculate the ratio and compare it to how it felt a quarter or a year ago.
Identify your highest-dependency workflow, the one that touches the most people, and see if you can cut the number of people it requires.
Rate Dependencies, Coord Time, Misalignment Probability, and Frequency 1-10 (see calculation, page 5)
Re-track quarterly, if the coordination ratio keeps climbing faster than output, your structure needs to change, not just your hustle.
content.explainer: This is why adding people can slow you down. Growth taxes itself through coordination overhead.
questions: What % of your work week goes to alignment instead of output? Is that ratio getting worse as you grow?
Which recurring dependency between two people or teams creates the most back-and-forth before anything ships?
If you added one more person to your busiest workflow tomorrow, would it speed things up or just add another meeting?
content.equation_example: Rate Dependencies, Coord Time, Misalignment Probability, and Frequency 1-10 each. Dependencies 6, Coord Time 7, Misalignment Probability 4, Frequency 5 → CT = 6×7×4×5 = 840 out of a possible 10,000. Higher CT means growth is being eaten by coordination overhead; lower means you're adding people without adding drag.
content.closing_statement: Adding people can slow you down, plan for it.

caption:
Principle Nº 24: Coordination Tax

As complexity rises, more value gets consumed by coordination itself. This is why adding people can slow you down.

Ask yourself: What % of your work week goes to alignment instead of output? Is that ratio getting worse as you grow?
